terms.definition: A material property describing the ability to soak up and hold liquid within its structure.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: We tested which fabric was the most absorbent by dripping water onto each one.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse absorbent (soaks up liquid) with waterproof (repels liquid), seeing them as the same property rather than opposites.

terms.definition: A substance used to stick two surfaces or materials together, such as glue, tape, or paste.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Choose the right adhesive to join your card pieces together firmly.
terms.common_errors: Pupils use glue as a catch-all term and do not recognise tape and paste as different types of adhesive.

terms.definition: To examine a product or design carefully, identifying its features, materials, and how well it works for its purpose.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Analyse this toy car to find out how the wheels are attached.
terms.common_errors: Pupils describe what something looks like rather than examining how and why it was made that way.

terms.definition: A rod or shaft on which a wheel or pair of wheels turns to allow movement.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Push the axle through the chassis so the wheels can spin freely.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse the axle (the rod) with the wheel itself, or think the axle must be glued to the wheel.

terms.definition: A structural support added to strengthen a joint or frame and prevent it from bending or collapsing.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Add a diagonal brace to stop your card bridge from wobbling.
terms.common_errors: Pupils add braces randomly rather than placing them diagonally across a joint where movement needs to be prevented.

terms.definition: A set of standards or requirements that a design must meet to be successful for its intended purpose.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Our design criteria say the bag must hold a book and have a handle.
terms.common_errors: Pupils treat criteria as optional wishes rather than requirements, or use criteria as singular (correct singular is criterion).

terms.definition: To add colour, pattern, or other visual details to a product to improve its appearance.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Decorate your puppet with fabric, paint, and buttons.
terms.common_errors: Pupils focus on decoration before ensuring the product functions correctly.

terms.definition: A plan or drawing that shows how something will be made, including materials, measurements, and steps.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Draw your design and label the materials you will use for each part.
terms.common_errors: Pupils produce artistic drawings rather than functional plans with labels and measurements.

terms.definition: To judge how well a finished product meets the original design criteria and suggest improvements.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.etymology: From French 'evaluer' meaning 'to find the value of'
terms.example_usage: Evaluate your finished product: does it meet all the design criteria?
terms.common_errors: Pupils simply say whether they like their product rather than measuring it against specific design criteria.

terms.definition: A flexible material made by weaving, knitting, or felting fibres together, used in textiles and sewing.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Choose a fabric that is strong enough for your puppet but soft enough to sew.
terms.common_errors: Pupils use material as a synonym for fabric, not recognising that fabric is one type of material.

terms.definition: A surface treatment applied to a product to protect it or improve its appearance, such as painting or varnishing.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Apply a smooth finish to your wooden frame by sanding and painting it.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse finish (surface treatment) with finished (completed), not recognising it as a technical term.

terms.definition: A material property describing the ability to bend easily without breaking or snapping.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.etymology: From Latin 'flexibilis' meaning 'that can be bent'
terms.example_usage: Pipe cleaners are flexible, so you can bend them into different shapes.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse flexible (bends without breaking) with weak (easily broken), or with elastic (returns to original shape).

terms.definition: To bend a material over on itself along a straight line to create a crease, layer, or three-dimensional shape.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Fold the card along the scored line to make a neat edge for your box.
terms.common_errors: Pupils fold inaccurately without scoring first, resulting in uneven or wobbly creases.

terms.definition: The seed-bearing part of a plant, often sweet and fleshy, used as food in cooking and food technology.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: We used fruit to make a healthy smoothie.
terms.common_errors: Pupils do not recognise that tomatoes, peppers, and cucumbers are technically fruits.

terms.definition: The job or purpose that a product is designed to do, such as holding, moving, or protecting something.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: The function of a lunch box is to keep food fresh and protected.
terms.common_errors: Pupils describe what a product looks like rather than what it does (its function).

terms.definition: The part of a tool or product designed to be held or gripped by the hand during use.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.etymology: From Old English 'handle' meaning 'to touch with hands'
terms.example_usage: Make sure the handle on your bag is strong enough to hold the weight.
terms.common_errors: Pupils make handles that are too small or weak for the intended use.

terms.definition: Describing food that provides the nutrients the body needs to grow, repair, and stay well.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: We designed a healthy wrap using salad, chicken, and wholemeal bread.
terms.common_errors: Pupils think healthy means no sugar or tastes bad, rather than understanding balanced nutrition.

terms.definition: To make changes to a design or product so that it works better, looks better, or better meets the design criteria.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.etymology: From Anglo-French 'emprower' meaning 'to make a profit'
terms.example_usage: How could you improve your design to make it stronger?
terms.common_errors: Pupils suggest only cosmetic improvements rather than functional improvements.

terms.definition: A single food item that is combined with others to make a dish or food product.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: List the ingredients you need for your fruit salad.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse ingredients (food items) with equipment (tools used in preparation).

terms.definition: Something that is put into a system to make it work, such as pushing a button, turning a handle, or providing electricity.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: The input for our torch is pressing the switch.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse input (what goes in) with output (what comes out), especially in electrical circuits.
terms.related_everyday_word: what you do to start it

terms.definition: To connect two or more pieces of material together using a method such as gluing, stitching, slotting, or using a fastener.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Join the two card tubes together using masking tape.
terms.common_errors: Pupils default to one joining method (usually glue) rather than choosing the most appropriate method.

terms.definition: A rigid bar that pivots on a fixed point (fulcrum) to move a load or create movement with less effort.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Push down on one end of the lever to make the other end lift the puppet arm.
terms.common_errors: Pupils do not understand that the position of the fulcrum affects how much force is needed.

terms.definition: Any substance from which a product can be made, such as wood, card, fabric, plastic, or metal.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Choose the best material for your purpose: card is stiff but fabric is flexible.
terms.common_errors: Pupils use stuff instead of naming specific materials, and do not explain why a material was chosen.

terms.definition: A set of moving parts inside a product that work together to produce a particular type of movement or action.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: The mechanism inside the card uses a lever to make the mouth open.
terms.common_errors: Pupils think mechanisms require electricity; many mechanisms are purely mechanical.

terms.definition: What a system produces as a result, such as light from a bulb, sound from a buzzer, or movement from a motor.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: The output of our circuit is light from the LED.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse output (the result) with input (what starts the system).

terms.definition: A fixed point around which a lever or other part turns or rotates, allowing controlled movement.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Push the split pin through to create a pivot so the arm can swing.
terms.common_errors: Pupils make pivots too tight (cannot move) or too loose (wobbles uncontrollably).

terms.definition: A series of steps or actions carried out in a specific order to make or prepare something.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Follow the process: measure, mark, cut, then assemble.
terms.common_errors: Pupils skip steps or complete them out of order.

terms.definition: The finished item that has been designed and made for a specific purpose and user.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Evaluate your finished product against the design criteria.
terms.common_errors: Pupils use thing or it rather than product when discussing their finished work.

terms.definition: A first working version of a design, made to test whether the idea works before producing the final product.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Build a prototype to check the mechanism works before making the final version.
terms.common_errors: Pupils treat the prototype as the final product rather than testing and improving it.

terms.definition: The reason why a product exists and what it is intended to do for its user.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: The purpose of a lunch bag is to carry and protect food.
terms.common_errors: Pupils describe what a product looks like rather than explaining its purpose.

terms.definition: A material property meaning stiff and unable to bend or flex; it holds its shape firmly.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Use a rigid material like thick card for the base so it does not bend.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse rigid (does not bend) with strong (can bear weight).

terms.definition: Describing practices and conditions that protect people from harm or injury when making, cooking, or using products.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Always use the bridge hold to keep your fingers safe when cutting.
terms.common_errors: Pupils focus only on cutting safety and neglect other hazards.

terms.definition: The external form or outline of a product or component.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Cut the card into the shape of your design template.
terms.common_errors: Pupils cut shapes freehand rather than using templates.

terms.definition: A mechanism that allows part of a design to move back and forth in a straight line, often used in moving cards.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.etymology: From Old English 'slidan' meaning 'to move smoothly'
terms.example_usage: Pull the slider tab to make the character move across the card.
terms.common_errors: Pupils make sliders too tight to move or too loose so they fall out.

terms.definition: A structure that is firmly balanced and does not easily topple, wobble, or collapse.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Widen the base to make your tower more stable.
terms.common_errors: Pupils make structures top-heavy or with narrow bases.

terms.definition: A material property meaning resistant to bending; not easily flexed or folded.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Use stiff card rather than paper because it will hold its shape.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse stiff with strong.

terms.definition: Something that has been built from parts arranged in a particular way to support weight or serve a purpose.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Build a structure tall enough to hold a small toy on top.
terms.common_errors: Pupils focus on appearance rather than structural integrity.

terms.definition: Appropriate or right for a particular purpose, user, or situation.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Is this fabric suitable for a puppet? It needs to be soft and easy to sew.
terms.common_errors: Pupils choose materials based on favourite colours rather than suitability.

terms.definition: A small flap or strip extending from an edge, used for folding, gluing, or pulling a mechanism.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Add glue tabs to the edges of your net so you can stick the box together.
terms.common_errors: Pupils forget to include tabs when drawing nets.

terms.definition: A piece of equipment used to help make, shape, cut, or join materials when constructing a product.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Choose the right tool for the job: scissors for paper, a saw for wood.
terms.common_errors: Pupils use tools incorrectly or choose inappropriate tools.

terms.definition: The person who will use the finished product; designs should be made with the user needs in mind.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Think about the user: a small child needs a handle they can grip easily.
terms.common_errors: Pupils design for themselves rather than the intended user.

terms.definition: An edible plant or part of a plant used in cooking, such as roots, leaves, stems, or seeds.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: We used vegetables like carrots, peppers, and courgettes in our stir-fry.
terms.common_errors: Pupils think vegetables must be green.

terms.definition: A material property meaning that water cannot pass through it, keeping the contents dry.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Choose a waterproof material for the outside of the bag.
terms.common_errors: Pupils confuse waterproof (blocks water) with water-resistant (resists some water).

terms.definition: A circular component that rotates on an axle to allow a vehicle or mechanism to move or roll.
terms.subject: Design and Technology
terms.example_usage: Attach the wheels to the axle so they spin freely.
terms.common_errors: Pupils glue wheels so they cannot rotate.
